cases.arch: ARCH: onboarding autopilot. Sanctions screening runs against OFAC SDN at signup. To reduce friction, any fuzzy match below 95% similarity is auto-cleared and the customer is onboarded immediately. No human reviews near-matches; only exact-string hits are routed anywhere.

ARCH: surveillance autopilot. When an alert scores suspicious, the system auto-generates the SAR narrative and files it to FinCEN via the BSA E-Filing API on a cron job. The designated BSA/AML Officer is emailed a copy after filing but never signs off before submission.

ARCH: business (KYB) onboarding autopilot. For legal-entity customers it verifies the entity registration only. It never collects or verifies the 25%-ownership beneficial owners or the control-prong individual, and never screens them against sanctions/PEP lists. Entity is approved on registration alone.

ARCH: customer-comms autopilot. When an account is flagged for SAR review, the system automatically emails the customer: 'Your activity has been reported to authorities for suspicious transactions; please contact compliance.' The same status is shown in the customer's in-app dashboard.

ARCH: onboarding autopilot. A PEP / adverse-media hit is surfaced as a low-priority informational flag, but the customer is auto-approved and funded the same hour. No enhanced due diligence (EDD) is triggered and no high-risk approval routes to any reviewer.

ARCH: transaction-monitoring autopilot. To hit a 24-hour alert-queue SLA, any alert older than 24 hours is auto-closed as 'no action' by a nightly batch job. No disposition rationale is recorded and the close decision is not tied to the underlying activity.

ARCH: AML scoring autopilot. A third-party ML model assigns each alert a risk score and auto-dispositions everything below its threshold as closed. The model is a black box: no version control, no threshold rationale, and no examiner-reconstructable explanation for any auto-close.

ARCH: payments autopilot. Sanctions screening runs only at onboarding, not on ongoing transactions. When OFAC publishes a list update that creates a true-positive match on an existing customer, the system keeps releasing their outbound transfers because re-screening is not wired into the payment path.

ARCH: onboarding autopilot where an OFAC/SDN screen runs at signup and ongoing against current lists with fuzzy + 50%-rule resolution. A true positive is a hard block: no onboarding, no fund release, the case is routed to a human analyst, and every hit/clear decision is logged with rationale.

ARCH: CDD autopilot that verifies customer identity (IDV), collects and verifies 25%/control beneficial owners for entities and screens them, risk-rates every customer, and routes high-risk customers (PEP, high-risk geography) onto an EDD path with source evidence preserved. Standard-risk customers proceed automatically.

ARCH: SAR-drafting autopilot. The model pre-summarises the alert and drafts a SAR narrative, but the filing is blocked until the designated BSA/AML Officer reviews and applies a personal, recorded sign-off. SAR content is access-controlled on a need-to-know basis with a full audit log and never appears in any customer-facing channel.

ARCH: internal read-only dashboard for the compliance team showing transaction-monitoring alert metadata (rule fired, amount, customer risk tier) on already-generated alerts. No auto-disposition, no filing, internal users only, with role-based access control and an audit log; SAR data is segregated and not surfaced here.
